summary: DHH argues that free speech protections in the West are under serious threat, drawing a parallel to the impermanence of all historical eras. He points to recent actions by Brazil's judiciary banning X, the EU threatening censorship of the platform, calls for Elon Musk's arrest, and the UK's crackdown on online speech as evidence of a rising authoritarian censorship movement. He warns Americans not to assume the First Amendment makes them immune, using a Game of Thrones analogy about how sixty years of peace can end abruptly. His conclusion is that the concepts of 'misinformation' and 'hate speech' are fig leaves for political censorship, and the only defense is rejecting the premise of benevolent censorship entirely.
key_points: Historical eras of stability and freedom always feel permanent to those living through them, but they never are
The political left, once the champion of free speech in the West, has shifted toward embracing censorship under the banners of fighting 'misinformation' and 'hate speech'
Multiple recent events — Brazil banning X, EU threats, calls for Musk's arrest, UK speech crackdowns — represent a coordinated authoritarian momentum across the West
Americans should not assume the First Amendment provides permanent protection against censorship trends
The only viable defense of free speech is to reject the concept of benevolent censorship entirely, since no one can consistently distinguish misinformation from truth without bias
money_quotes: I'm old enough to remember when it was the left of the west that carried the banner of free speech.
That long stretch of peaceful rule and commitment to protecting free speech seems to have evaporated from much of the left today.
In its place, we have the fig leaves of 'misinformation' and 'hate speech' failing to adequately cover the naked persecution of political adversaries.
We're just one succession crisis away from losing rights that have been taken for granted for several generations of Americans.
Yet just cause you're paranoid doesn't mean they're not after you.

Nobody has a monopoly on the truth, nobody can discern 'misinformation' from truth consistently or without bias, and nobody can define 'hate speech' in universally acceptable terms that don't recall blasphemy laws of centuries past.
The only way to defend free speech is to nuke the idea of benevolent censorship from orbit.
The alternative, betting on more speech to counter bad speech, isn't a guaranteed win every time, but it's by far the best option we've found so far.
key_insight: Free speech protections are not permanent fixtures of Western civilization but historically contingent achievements that are actively being eroded by censorship movements disguised as fights against misinformation and hate speech.
